Boiling Point The normal boiling point is “the temperature at which a liquid boils at
atmospheric pressure.” The boiling points of hydrocarbons are related to the
number of carbon atoms. The more carbon atoms, the higher the boiling point.
The difference in boiling points makes possible the separation of different
hydrocarbons (distillation and fractionation).

Charles Law states that at a Constant Pressure, if the absolute
temperature is doubled, then its volume will also be doubled.
For any isobaric (same pressure) process, the volume of a given mass of gas is directly
proportional to its absolute temperature (refer to Figure 6).

Combined Gas Law
Boyle’s Law and Charles’ Law refer to “isothermal” and “isobaric” changes, respectively. However,
during many gas processes, the gas pressure and temperature change simultaneously. Therefore, it
is desirable to use a formula that relates to the three variables of pressure, temperature, and volume.
The relationship between these is referred to as the Combined Gas Law, which is written:

A comparison to the properties of LNG to those of other liquid fuels, as shown in Table 3, also
indicates that the lower flammability limit of LNG is generally higher than other fuels. That is, more
LNG vapors would be needed (in a given area) to ignite as compared to LPG or gasoline.

designed and operated using standards and procedures to eliminate this hazard, and are equipped
with extensive fire detection and protection systems should flames or sparks occur.

Calculating Who is at Risk
The maximum vapor cloud fire hazard area from large LNG spills is typically estimated by calculating
a downwind dispersion distance to the lower flammable limit (LFL) and a crosswind dispersion
distance to 1/2 LFL at low wind speed and stable atmospheric conditions. This maximum fire hazard
zone is very unlikely to be experienced in any situation where the cloud drifts overpopulated areas.
